params.code:
# AN ELEMENTARY CELLULAR AUTOMATON. A row of cells, each alive or dead. To build the
# next row, every cell looks at THREE cells above it (itself and its two neighbours)
# and asks the rule what to become. Eight possible neighbourhoods -> eight answers
# -> the rule is one byte. That is the entire program: 8 bits.
rule = 30         #@param int min=0 max=255
generations = 22  #@param int min=1 max=40
width = 41        #@param int min=9 max=81
start = 0         #@param int min=0 max=1
seed = 3          #@param int min=1 max=999
cell = 4.0        #@param float min=1 max=10 step=0.5
R, W, G = int(rule), int(width), int(generations)
rng = random.Random(int(seed))
# start = 0: ONE live cell in the middle. Everything that follows comes from there.
# start = 1: a random row — same rule, and it still organises itself.
row = [0] * W
if start == 0:
    row[W // 2] = 1
else:
    row = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(W)]
alive = []
for g in range(G):
    for i, c in enumerate(row):
        if c:
            alive.append((i, g))
    nxt = []
    for i in range(W):
        left = row[(i - 1) % W]         # the row wraps around, like a ring
        me = row[i]
        right = row[(i + 1) % W]
        idx = (left << 2) | (me << 1) | right       # the neighbourhood, as 0..7
        nxt.append((R >> idx) & 1)                  # the rule, read bit by bit
    row = nxt
# each live cell becomes a cube. Generations stack ALONG Z: the tower is TIME.
result = {"alive": alive, "W": W, "G": G, "cell": cell, "rule": R,
          "n": len(alive)}

# one point per LIVE cell: x = position in the row, z = which generation.
# Dead cells produce nothing — the empty space IS part of the pattern.
D = in_0
c, W = D["cell"], D["W"]
result = [Vector((i - W / 2) * c, 0, g * c + c / 2) for i, g in D["alive"]]

size = 4.0  #@param float min=2 max=12 step=0.5
D = in_0
c, W, G, R = D["cell"], D["W"], D["G"], D["rule"]
famous = {30: "chaos (used as a random generator)",
          90: "the Sierpinski triangle, out of nothing",
          110: "Turing complete: this can compute anything",
          184: "traffic jams", 250: "a simple weave", 54: "gliders and collisions"}
def _tv(s, x, z, fs):        # standing, facing the camera
    return extrude(Pos(x, -12, z) * (Plane.XZ * Text(s, font_size=fs)), amount=0.8)
top = G * c + 14
result = [_tv("ELEMENTARY CELLULAR AUTOMATON", 0, top + 14, size * 1.5),
          _tv(f"rule {R}   =   {R:08b}", 0, top, size * 1.4),
          _tv(famous.get(R, "a rule like any other: 8 bits"), 0, top - 12, size),
          _tv("time  ->", -W * c / 2 - 16, G * c / 2, size)]

params.text:
EIGHT BITS OF PROGRAM, AND A UNIVERSE.

The bottom row is the start: ONE live cell. Each row above it is the next generation, so the tower grows upward through TIME — and the whole history is one solid you could print.

The rule is the entire program. A cell looks at three cells (itself and its two neighbours): eight possible neighbourhoods, eight yes/no answers. Eight bits. One byte. That is all there is — no memory, no arithmetic, no randomness.

TRY THIS — drag `rule`:
rule 90: a perfect Sierpinski triangle. A fractal, out of one cell and a byte.
rule 30: chaos. It is so unpredictable that it has been used as a random number generator — yet nothing here is random. Run it again and you get the same chaos, exactly.
rule 110: this one is TURING COMPLETE. Those little gliders drifting and colliding can carry out any computation a computer can. Eight bits.
rule 250, 184, 54: weaves, traffic jams, colliding particles.

Then set `start` to 1: a random first row. Same rules, and they still organise themselves — the order was never in the seed, it was in the rule.
